text: During a thoracoscopic right lower lobectomy, the surgeon identifies the anomalous artery of the sequestered lung within the inferior pulmonary ligament, initially mistaken for dense adhesions.
It was injured by the electrical regulation hook when we dissected the ligament.
The surgeon places two pairs of curved color forceps on the surgical field to control bleeding, securing the vessels or tissue causing the hemorrhage.
The surgeon replaces the distal curved color forceps with a titanium clip to create adequate space for the insertion of the endostapler.
The surgeon uses an endostapler to transect the vascular bundle, completing the separation of the targeted vessels.
During the thoracoscopic rhizopalobectomy for pulmonary adenocarcinoma, the truncus anterior is visibly injured by the electrocoagulation hook.
The surgeon controls bleeding at the injured site using endoscopic suction and applies site compression, followed by a re-evaluation of the injury.

A pair of curved scallop forceps is introduced to clamp the hole carefully for bleeding control. The superior pulmonary vein is transected to improve exposure of the injured artery. Subsequently, the injured truncus anterior is sutured to ensure the safety of the operation.
The surgeon performs a suture on the injured area while controlling bleeding by applying side compression with a suction device.
The surgeon precisely controls the bleeding at the truncus anterior before further dissecting the vessel and transecting it using an endostapler.

The surgeon sews a stitch on the lymph node adhered tightly to the superior vena cava for retraction, then performs sharp dissection using a pair of scissors to separate the lymph node from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon accidentally makes a five-millimeter cut on the wall of the superior vena cava using scissors.
The surgeon uses the suction tip to compress the injured site on the superior vena cava, visually assessing the wound for any signs of bleeding or further injury.
The surgeon uses a routine non-needle holder to perform angiography, employing a 5-0 pulling stitch technique.
The surgeon performs an intraoperative pathological assessment on the frozen section of the dissected lymph nodes, revealing no metastasis. Consequently, part of the lymph node is left undissected.

During the thoracoscopic red epilobectomy, the surgeon accidentally injures an exogenous vein using an electrocoagulation hook. The wound is compressed with suction teeth introduced through the posterior port to control bleeding.
The surgeon performs further dissection to prepare the site for suturing. Using a routine needle holder, a five-zero purling stitch is applied to suture the wound through the anterior working port.

The surgeon performs the first suture on one side of the wound after slightly repositioning the suction tip to expose the area. The suction tip is then moved in the opposite direction to allow the second suture on the other side of the wound, followed by knot tying.
During the thoracoscopic left upper lobectomy, the surgeon transects the superior pulmonary vein and left upper lobe bronchus. Dense adhesions are observed surrounding the pulmonary artery.
The surgeon carefully dissects the pulmonary artery to improve exposure of the lingual artery, revealing the anatomical structures and their spatial relationships.
During the blunt dissection of the lingual artery, an accidental laceration occurs at the junction of the lingual artery and the main pulmonary artery, visible as a breach in the vessel wall.

The suction tip is introduced to compress the wound site for bleeding control, while the camera view is temporarily obscured by massive bleeding. The bleeding is successfully controlled through site compression using the suction.
The surgeon uses a pair of Alice forceps to clamp the vascular wound, replacing the suction tip for further manipulation.
The surgeon dissects the left main pulmonary artery and secures it with an endoscopic atraumatic vascular clamp.

The surgeon carefully inspects the laceration at the junction of the lingual artery and main pulmonary artery. Angiography is performed using a running 5-0 suture, with an anastopic needle holder introduced through the posterior working port.
The surgeon carefully avoids sewing up the contralateral wall of the artery during the procedure, ensuring precise manipulation of the vascular structures.
The surgeon removes the vascular clamp from the left main pulmonary artery after securing the knot, carefully inspecting the site for any signs of leakage.
The surgeon carefully dissects and transects the lingual artery, followed by the transection of the posterior ascending artery and the oblique fissure.
The surgeon removes the resected lobe using a retrieval bag fashioned from a rubber glove, ensuring the lobe is safely extracted from the surgical field.
